speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I don't know, I can talk into one of these things. I don't know. Just like we're talking like usual because it would be the same. I first wanted to ask you about the name of the town you grew up in and where it was located in Norway.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I grew up in a place they called, well you mean the real neighborhood?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well that was Ramsdale. Oh. That's the name of that. And then the nearest shopping place where they had a store and a church and a school. One of the steamers used to come in on the ocean and they called Eikefjord.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And it was about seven English miles I think from Ramsdale down to Eikefjord. Ramsdale was the last place in that valley. There's a valley run up through there in the river and two lakes in there in that valley. And the place where I came from was the last place. The end of the road right up against the mountain. There's a mountain right around like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This name, your last name is Ramsdale and that's the name of the place.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how it worked?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes, that's how it worked.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. I might tell you about my dad. He came from a place they called Solheim and his name was Solheim. But he married my mother and my mother was the only child there and she inherited the farm there, you know. And when dad, he changed his name from Solheim to Ramsdale. So I still have that name of the place of the neighborhood there. There's about four farmers there in that neighborhood with that name, same name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that, that's how it works? Each neighborhood has its own name and people who live there have that name too?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that was the way they had it in the old days.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And my brother there, Elias, he inherited the whole place there. And he had all the girls, he didn't have the boys.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So one of his daughters got the whole place that I grew up on and she married a fellow named Hesjedal. And then he changed his name to Ramsdale and he married her.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But still Ramsdale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I guess that's how much more important the land is there than here because they have to, your name go by the land.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, well they really think a lot of their home farms and the home places over there don't hardly ever go out of the family.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Where do you sell them?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They try to keep it in the family. Some of them don't operate the land but still they keep it, they keep it in the family. And it's hard to buy the land over there, there's hardly anything for sale. You never see any ads in the newspapers about land for sale or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you sell them?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, last summer I was over there and the neighbor there, he kind of wanted to sell his place. He didn't have any kids or anything, so I thought well, I'd like to buy that and then add it to the old home place to make it a bigger place. But they have a law there, you've got to be a citizen of the country to buy land there. So I couldn't buy it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big is the old home place?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, just a small place, the place over there is small. They have a lot of pasture land and waste land in the mountains around there. But the cultivated land is just patches on what they had there around. So, eight, ten acres cleared, and then they had milk cows, that's how they used to make their livings off of the farm's bacteria. They grow hay and a few oats. Vegetables grow good there, like potatoes and carrots and all kinds of vegetables, they grow real good there. And then the hay, they fed the cows and then sold the milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they grow the hay? Were there eight acres?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they grow enough there to feed six or eight cows.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Because they cut some of the wild hay there too, in the old days. They go up on the slopes, you know, on the mountains there and cut wild hay, grass, put it up. I was good sheep feed, everybody had a few sheep them days too. Had goats too when I left there. It sounds pretty much like the old days around here, the early days. Well, similar I guess in the beginning here. We used to cut the wool off of the sheep there and they'd spin it and they'd claw it out of it and they'd thread knitted socks and stuff on the wool off the sheep. And then, same thing, we'd butcher maybe a cow once in a while or a younger animal, yearling her. And then we'd take the hide and tan, that makes use of it. The leather. She made her own shoes. I didn't have any bought shoes like them over in this country.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: They went home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it take a long time to tan the leather and make shoes out of it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Not so very long. Well, first they let the hide dry, you know, kind of. Then they took it out to an old kind of a pawn they had there and you put the hide in the pawn. And you let it lay there until the hair slips. It actually kind of starts to decay. And the hair will slip. And you get all the hair off of it. And then you put it in and wax, you know, and put bark in there. You grind the bark. And then put it in there and I don't know how long they had the hide in there. Oh, probably a month or two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How kind of a fit did you get out of a pair of shoes like that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, good. Real good. That's good shoes. Yeah, they're very good with measuring the foot and making the last, you know, and all that. Just the whole thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it had like regular sole on it?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. We just wouldn't pegs, you know, for fasting the soles on instead of sewing them on. Had them pegs about that long, a little one. Throw them in and you took a rasp on the inside and they'd rub so they wouldn't slip out. They were good tights used. They were waterproof. That leather was real good. It really filled the water out. Because we had to grease it a lot, you know. Get fat into it. Used to grease off of towels, you know. We'd put you in a hog and you rub that grease into it. Got it soft.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a house did you have there?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, we had a pretty good house. Yeah, it was a pretty big house. We had a big living room and a kitchen and upstairs. And then we had another kind of living room that we didn't live in. You know, in them days they had a kind of special room that they didn't live in. Then if you got company or somebody stayed overnight or something, then they put them in that room there, you know. That was the company. Kept it nice all the time. The kids wasn't even, couldn't even go in there, you know. I got a picture of the house there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's my niece's daughter there. Got married at the wedding. So it's right up against the hill that was covered with mostly birch, timber. Some ash in there, mountain ash, but never old birch.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: In that neighborhood, like the Ramsdale there, there wasn't hardly any pine or anything. Birch and some ash.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Used to be. We used to dig ditches and then way back sometimes there'd been a lot of big pines there. Because we found them way down in the ground, big logs. That big. We used to be able to dig ditches that would drain some of them swamps, you know. Metal swamps there. And we'd dig down maybe, oh, 10 feet of ditches. And we'd run into them logs laying down in the pine logs. Pitchy, yellow pine logs. And there wasn't one of them on the ground there now. Not a one. So there'd been really a change there sometime around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use the birch wood?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's for firewood.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And we used the bark for tanning leather, but you know birch got two layers, you know. You probably know there's one on the outside layer, that tin little stuff there, and then there's a ticker bark under there. Well that outside layer, we used to make roofs out of that. That's the only kind of a roof we had when I was a kid. We'd take that off in the spring when it would slip, you know, in the spring it'd slip, see when the trees started growing. And we'd take that off and we'd put it in layers, you know, lay it out flat like this, and then we'd put rocks down there to keep it flat in big piles.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And that's what we used for roof on the barns and the hogs and everything. We'd lay that on there and then we'd take sod, slices of sod and lay on top of that to hold it down so the wind wouldn't blow it off. We had that on the house too, we had that on this house here, but later they took it off and put that rock roofing on there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it last for a long time?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, them roofs last a long time, oh, 20, 30 years. Yeah, they were good roofs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your water supply?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, a little spring out this way here that we got our water.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: You see the big rocks at that basement?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I remember when they built this house, I was just a little kid then. I remember the old house we had, I don't know how old it was, about five, probably five years old when this house was built.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did the lumber for it come from?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, down closer to the ocean there, they had lots of pine forests, you know. And that's where we got logs for the lumber.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: This house here or the other houses, the way they built them, they didn't have 2x4s. You know, like we have now, they just had maybe a log about 4 inches thick, you know. And they just put one on top of the oven and they cut off the outside of it, made boards out of it. So it was built out of 4 inch thick planks, you might say, you know. That's how they built them. And they put siding on the outside of boards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you were a little boy there, what did little kids do in Norway when they were growing up? Did you start school early?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, we started when I was seven years old.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: We had a school in homes then, we didn't have a school house. When I got old up there we had a school in private homes.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then they wouldn't have the school in the same home all the time, they would rotate it, you know. Then we have it down a little ways in the valley there one year, next year maybe up further, you know. And we only had, there wasn't many kids there, I don't remember just how many we had in school there, maybe 13 or 14 kids when I started school there. But then later on we built a new school house. There we go. And so we all went to school there. But we only had terms of school, we had two weeks of school there in that school house. And then the same teacher, he go down, further down in the valley and have school two weeks down there, see. So we had two weeks and then two weeks between each term.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That doesn't sound too bad.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But they had to do an awful lot of studying at home there, they gave us our lessons. And we had to learn that at home by heart, you know. When we came to school the next morning we had to know everything by heart. But we had, well the first hour we generally had Bible history. We had lesson that we had to know by heart and then we had catechism. You know that. We had class then we had arithmetic and history. And we had a And we had two hours a week singing, practice and singing. And then we had what we could call naturas, stories, I don't know what they call that. You know, learn about animals and nature and all that. We had all of that every day. We had pretty good school, really. You know. There was one thing there when we went to school, we couldn't get by with anything, you know. We really had to study and know our lessons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have a lot of books in the school? Were there books for the subjects or was it mostly just for the teacher?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Each one of us had a book and we take the book home to us. We had our own book. And we took the book home to us, learning lessons at home. It was all kids in one room just like the old school houses here. All in one room, all in one room.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about play?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, we had sports. We didn't have basketball or football or anything like that. But we had foot races and high jump and long jump. And a lot of games, you know, we played in school. And we had tree planting every spring. We had a whole school that got out for a couple of days and planted trees. Little trees. That was quite interesting. Some of the trees that I helped plant when I was a kid in school there, they were harvesting now when I was back there. Yeah. That was kind of interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have any horses on the farm?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, we had horses. Well, generally one or two. Sometimes we had a broodmare, you know, and they'd raise a colt each year. We used a horse there. Of course, we had horse and buggy, you know. We used a single horse to drive there. We didn't use two horses that they did here. Well, some of them used single here too, you know. But we only had one horse and a small buggy that we used when we went to town or to church or wherever we went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were at home, not at school, were you expected to work most of the time or did you play most of the time?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, we done a lot of playing again. Of course, we had to work too when there was work to do. Yeah, out in the woods, you know, we used to go up to them steep mountains like getting on firewood and we had to drag it down there by hand there for quite a ways until we'd get at it with a horse to haul it. And then in the summertime we'd cut that wild hay way back up in the mountains. Then we packed it down, you know. We had a little building there to put it in but most of that hay was packed in, put in that building. Kind of rough, you couldn't get at it with a horse.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So we had a chores to do, all right.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But then we played a lot too, you know. Kids get together and play and have games.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then we had a young people's organization there like, I know we had a tamper's organization there that we belonged to and we had meetings and we had plays there sometimes. Programs.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So we had a pretty good time, really.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, we had that here too when I came here first. We had a lot of home entertainments here. Like we had parties in the wintertime, we used to go from house to house and have parties. And we had a livery, we had that in the schoolhouse and we had plays there and a lot of things going on. And then the Bethe Church down here, it was kind of a center for this community here, you know. And we had choir down there and choir practice about twice a week, we had choir practice. And choir there and singing. Summertime they had picnics and made ice cream, I remember. They turned that old ice cream freezer a lot, you know, and that old ice cream, you know. That was really good, you know, that homemade ice cream. Used to put ice and salt in there. Used to put up ice here in the water time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, you cut them square blocks off of the ponds or wherever you had water in the throws, maybe. You know, you take a saw and saw that ice into square blocks and then put it in the building and put sawdust all around it. And that ice would keep there for the next summer if you put enough sawdust around it. It'd be iced there all summer. I didn't know that they did that here. Yeah, we did that here. And that's the ice they used to use when they had ice cream.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you do that in a group, getting the ice?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, most of the time. A few of them went together and worked together. They used to work together quite a bit, you know, in the old days here like butchering, you know. Most of them would butcher in the fall like butcher hogs. Most of the time they'd butcher four or five hogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then they'd get together, see?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Butcher hogs and they'd do it all up in one day. Cut them up and solder down or sugar cure them and then ram the lard and make the sausage and all that. And then one day the whole thing.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Do you think...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: what were you saying?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I was going to say the same thing about trashing here. They helped for a lot of trashing, drain, exchange work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it was a lot like here the way it was where you grew up?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Co -operating?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Quite a bit. Yeah, when I first came here it was quite a bit like the old country. Maybe a little farther advanced here. And back there they... it seemed like here this country's always kind of being ahead of the rest of the country, going ahead, advancing. And the rest of them finally followed. Like the automobile, you know. I think it first came here and then finally they got them everywhere now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But it seems to me like with such a small amount of land that you had there, there wasn't too much of it. It was hard, it'd be hard to get ahead in that country.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: It was hard to get ahead. People just made a living, that's about all. Pretty hard to get ahead much. But it seemed like they had a pretty good living back there. They always had plenty to eat. We had clothes, you know, and everything. We got along, but didn't have any money hardly, you know. The money was really scarce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the town of Eikefjord, what was that town like?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, it's just a small shopping place there. They had two stores I think we could buy. A few things, they didn't have many grocers in the stores. Those were the stores, them days only they bought some flour and sugar and coffee and salt and pepper and a few spices and a few more items. But they didn't have many varieties in the grocery stores, them days, like they have now. And they had a church there, that's where everybody in that neighborhood went to church. There's only one church in the whole area there, you know, Luther church. And the steamers come in there, brought the mail in there, and all the freight, whatever you needed, and people who would travel. They went by the steamers, the boats, they went into a bigger town, went down there to Eikefjord and then got on the boat and went to bigger cities.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And they had a school there too, a pretty good sized schoolhouse there, Eikefjord. And the graveyard, it was there, Eikefjord. And it was just a small place there. It's a little bit bigger now, and then they built a new school there. Now the whole valley goes down there at the school. They followed the same as they have here, they consolidated and now they have bus, golf, they all went down there to scoop them out, Eikefjord.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the herring run that you mentioned?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah, that was in the spring of the year, that was in Norwegian War, you know, that's spring. And the spring herring run, it was starting last January, February, March, and everybody used to try to go out on that to make a little money. So I'm about two seasons old, I was just a little kid, I was about 15 years old I guess, first time I went out. And those schools, you know, the herring, they come in to spawn in on the sand banks on the shore there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And there's a lot of other fish too, you know, lived on the herring like a whale, you can see the whale out there too, you know, because they wouldn't come in, in very close, but they were out there, you could see them. And then the herring come in, why we'd surround them with a net, you know, so they couldn't get away. The boss of our crew, there's a crew of about 12 I think, each crew was just about 12 men, and we lived on a boat, it's a sailboat. And the boss would be up on the hills on nights, you know, watching for his herring schools on a clear night, you can see the reflection in the sky, you know, from the herring, they come up very close to the surface of the water, and it reflects on the sky, something like the northern lights. And he'd watch that reflection where they went, and then if they got in close to land someplace, they probably could get them surrounded, why, he'd hurry down and get everybody out of bed and off they'd go, they'd have nets around them. And then we'd have them, they couldn't get away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you get the nets around them, did you wade the water?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, no rowboats, boats, boats, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: See the nets, you know, they have rocks on the bottom and cork on the top, so you throw them out and they make a wall, you know, one end goes down to the bottom and cork on the top holds, floats and won't sink.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then they'd get in touch with some of the buyers, at times I was out there, we sold the herring to Englishmen, they come in with the steamers, they're from England, and we'd load them up with herring, they'd take off, they'd put the herring in boxes, they had ice with them, so they used shovel to shovel the herring in, then the shovel of ice, they'd fill the box up, and some of them steamers held about two thousand boxes, the herring in the box would hold, oh, used to buy the barrel I guess. But then we worked night and day till we got the boat loaded, and everybody got together then too, you know, when the boat come in, and they want to get loaded just as soon as possible on account of the ice would melt I suppose. So you had to everybody get out and work then, get the boat loaded. So that was quite a business them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a catch would you come up with in one run in the rowboat, laying it out?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh hey, I think we had some catches there up too, oh I'd say two or three thousand barrels, you know, oh yeah, those big schools come in there. And that's the herring.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And once in a while we'd catch some cod too, or other fish in there with them. One time out there I seen the herring and the fish come in, and they were so tight they come up, and on the shore there, you know, the tide would, waves come in, you know, and the herring come in, then the wave go back out again, and they'd lay there and kick in the sand there and the bears would come out. And that's how they were crowded up against the land there. And we went out in the rowboat there with hooks, you know, and we'd hook a lot of cod fish right out of the rowboat. They were right up in the surface of the tide in there, you know. Just packed in there. We'd catch them just with a hook.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long did this run last?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, we used to figure on a month and a half, two months. The way they actually worked then, they started up in the northern part there earlier. Then later on, why they seemed like they'd move south, and we'd move with them, you know. We'd go down south and maybe we'd catch another school down there. So we used to be out there for a month and a half, two months.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean you'd leave Eikefjord to go further south?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: On a sailboat. We all lived on a sailboat. We took, I'll tell you what kind of grub we had with us, we took grub steak with us. We'd buy a whole barrel full of hardtacks, you know, we call them cobring. That's a dry toast, it's really a toast, you know, made out of rye. But it would keep, it'd dry, so we'd buy a whole barrel full of that. And then we'd have a big slab of pork, maybe. And then we'd buy a sack or two of peas. We had lots of pea soup. That was a good dish them days, back then, pea soup. We'd have a pan and a little pork. And then we'd cook mush, you know, and stuff like that. Had other meat too with us, like beef. We'd cook lots of potatoes, vegetables like rutabagas, carrots. Had a whole supply with us on the boat, right on the boat there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you just lived on the boat?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Lived on the boat, just slept on the boat, cooked there, and eat there and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you were ready to go night or day when the fish came?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That's right. We had to be ready to go any notice we got, had to be ready to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what happened if you didn't have a good wind when the fish came? You were finished?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, I think it was only once that we had to be tugged. They had, the rest of the fellows had tug boats, you know, to pull the other boats in case you didn't have any wind so you could move one place to the other. Why they had those little tug boats that pulled you. And that happened once, I think. We had to be pulled by a tug boat. Didn't have enough wind to sail.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the ship owned by one man?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, sometimes there was two or three, four of them in there, ownership. And then of course the way the money was divided up, the boat got so big a share and then the owners, they got so much percentage and then the workers, you know, common working man gets a certain percentage of the catch, you know. That's the way it was. So if you didn't catch anything, you didn't get anything.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But the first year I was hot, we hadn't been lucky. I made a hundred and sixty crowns there and that was good them days. Really pretty big money. I know fellows worked the whole year for less than that, you know. So it was, just depends on how, how the herring run was. That's what it hinged on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be about the best way a young fellow could make money in that country?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yes, that was about the best. If you haven't been lucky.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Because the wages there were really low. I knew a young fellow there about my age that he worked there for my neighbor there for a year for that. Now he was supposed to get a hundred crowns for a year's worth. And then of course he got his board and he likes to stay and maybe he got a few clothes with it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did this hundred crowns amount to then? An American?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, American money amounted to about twenty -five dollars.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Just about four crowns to the dollar them days.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, of course that's the reason why there's many, many people immigrating over here them days because the wages are much better here than back in them old countries. It's a reason. We all hear about America, you know, how they got rich over here, got big wages, you know, that. Everybody want to come here.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And them days there was lots of work there too, you know. They were even advertising back there for labor, you know, the railroads and the big logging companies and all that. They were advertising for labor back in that country. No kidding. They advertised right in Norway for people. Did you ever see those advertisements when you were... Yeah, like the railroads, you know. They were building railroads here then. They need lots of men, you know. They need advertising for men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in your people that you knew, who were the first ones who had come over to this country from Norway?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I don't know. The first one that I... I don't remember that was my uncle, my dad's brother. He came over here. Well, I don't know what year it was, but it must have been in the 1800s. Of course, these Wayan brothers here, you know, Olsen brothers, they came over before then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They left there in what?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: 1865 maybe, something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were from the neighborhood too?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, they were from the other side of the mountain, fairly close.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Just over the hill there by the hour or two is walked, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Then was it your brother who came over to the United States?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, my brother was over here for five years before I came.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He came back home before I left. I guess he was home there for a couple of years before I came over here. Yeah, he worked in North Dakota first for a farmer, and then he went to Superior, Wisconsin and worked for my uncle there. My uncle had a little grocery store there, at Superior. And my brother worked for him there as a delivery boy, I think, to deliver groceries. Thank you. And then he came back home again and he got married, settled on him, stayed over there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Back in Norway?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. Well, he was the one that got the home place. He was the oldest one in the family, so he inherited the home, home place, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, this was usual for people to come over and then come back?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, quite usual. A lot of them did that. Come over here and made a little stake and then go back home again. Well, I had that mind too, you know, when I came over. Oh yeah, I didn't tend to come over here to stay when I left over there. I just thought I'd come over here, make some money on the stake, and then go back home again to stay home. But, I don't know. I made my first trip home when I was in 1920. I was kind of half ways. I decided to stay home. But I couldn't find anything over there like to do. I didn't want to farm over there. Of course, it was hard to get into anything anyhow. I went to work for wedges and those jobs and they paid so little, I just kind of felt like it was a waste of time to stay there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, I decided to come back here again.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How far is Eikefjord from the city?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, from Fløre, probably about 20 miles. That was the nearest town. That wasn't a big town either. But, then Bergen is a bigger town. That's a pretty good sign. It was a town there and that was about 20 Norwegian miles from where we lived. That would be, what, 7, yeah, 140, that would be the English miles. It was a big city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, the idea of going to the United States had a lot of influence or attraction on people when you grew up. It did. It really did.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: There's a lot of people immigrating about the time I did. Just a lot of them from all over, everywhere. Well, they had so many immigrants come over here that they had an immigrant train running from New York out west that had nothing but immigrants on it. It's a fact. There was an immigrant train that I got on when I came over here and they were all immigrants on that train to start with. And then, of course, as they went they kept on loading, you know, until you finally get down to a few like that car I was in. I remember there was just two of us left. There was a girl and me.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And, I went over and I was wondering if I could talk to her, but I couldn't understand her. She couldn't understand me. She was a Russian girl. And, we just kind of laughed at each other.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But, those seats, you know, they didn't have any cushions. They were 2x4's, you know. We said they were 2x4's. Well, there was all kinds. There was an awful lot of pins on that boat I came under and a lot of Russians. An awful lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did people really believe that they could get ahead in the United States? And, that was the reports that they had?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Definitely. And, that's the reason why some men are immigrated because they... I heard they can make a lot of money over here, you know. High wages. Lots of work. In fact, their work is scarce too. Couldn't always get a job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I got some things you like, Steve?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Sure. A little piece. That'd be good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you on coming over here? What made you decide to come here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I decided that, well, I had to get out and work. You couldn't stay home all the time. So, I decided I could make money a whole lot faster over here than I could over there. So, I thought I'd take a chance. Come over here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you wind up getting in contact with these Wilson brothers?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, they had a brother back there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: His name was Old McCall. He lived close to where we lived.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, we got him to write over here and ask him if we could come here if they thought we could get a job here, you know, and stuff like that. So, we got an answer. They thought we could work and we could come to them, you know, use their name. You had to get somebody here, some name to use that you'd come to here in Wisconsin. So, we used their name when I came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Any reason you picked coming here instead of to Wisconsin or where your uncle had been?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I was kind of farming, you know, I kind of wanted to farm, you know, and I kind of had that in my life. When I went out fishing there at the time, I didn't like to be in the water too good, you know, so I thought I'd rather go for something else, you know, than be a sailor. You know, one of those days I'd say, you know, I'd rather go for some kind of a land job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard to get clearance to come to this country? Is all you needed to have someone to speak for you?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: That was easy. Oh yeah, no problem at all. You didn't have to have the passports or anything. Just have a name here, somebody to come to here. It's about all, buy a ticket.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your trip like getting over?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, it took about three weeks. We went by boat to Bergen and then we got on a statement from Bergen to Hull, England.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then we took the train from Hull over to Liverpool across England.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But we actually missed the boat that we were supposed to get on to come over on. I don't know whether we got delayed or something, but anyhow we missed it. We had to stay there in Liverpool then for three days before we got on the boat to come over here.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Otherwise it was a nice trip, nice weather. Sitting up on the deck in the sun every day. Really nice. Ocean is just like a looking glass.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It sounds like you were lucky some people came over. Oh, I was really lucky there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I've been across there when we never got on deck at all. We stayed down under on the whole trip. Water, ocean was going over the deck every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you tried to start learning English when you were in Liverpool? Did you mention that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, yeah. Well, mine and other neighbors' kids there, you know, stayed together.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: We stopped on the sidewalk there in Liverpool and it was a tame big horses pulled up there. They used to freight by horses then they didn't have their trucks. We never seen big horses like that back in Norway and they had a small fjord hest they called.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Fjord hest?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, fjord hest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does that mean?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, fjords, you know, you don't have many fjords in Norway and then the hest has horse, you know. Well, there's something like a welt, I guess. More like than anything else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So these were big horses?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: These were big, big showers, you know, with long hair in the legs. So we stopped there and a guy got out and went in the store there. So I said to my partner, let's stay here now and hear what he says when they come back out to make them horses go, you know. So we stood there and waited till they come back out. Got in this rig and we set it up, you know. By the way, we caught that word, you know, I kept rotating that over and over and over to the stuff I didn't forget. That was the first word of English I remember that I learned.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think? Did you come in New York? Is that where you landed? What did you think of that?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, we of course we noticed that Statue of Liberty there, you know, that's more than the first thing we noticed there. What a huge thing that was.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, we then, of course we didn't get out and they rushed us, you know, to the railroad, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Didn't get to see very much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: See, straight to here you came by railroad, straight to here.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You told me that Nick and John lived right over the hill here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, they live in that cabin up there on the top there when I come over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The one that you can see from here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's where I first came to, right up in that cabin there. Too bad that that went down, it should have been taken care of I guess.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah. And when that was built there it was built in two buildings to start with. Of course the line between these two homes there went right through the middle of that cabin up there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And they left it open after they got proved up and then they built it together.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They just joined the two houses?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, just joined together.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Made it into one.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, you better have some more of this cheese, it's getting old, that's how I've had it since Christmas here. It's good cheese. Yeah, it is pretty good cheese.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Man. I think they make this up a day in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you got here what happened then? Did they just put you to work?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I went out and feed the pitched hay the next day. Got here just in hay in time and then after hay. They boarded with the farmers, you know, and everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened with the people who were, like these people who worked for you and they had no work anymore, there wasn't any work for them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: These guys that were the bundle haulers and those people, they still stayed in the area?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh, well, they hunted for work, you know. Oh no, a lot of them left again, you know. Well, people who hunt for work, of course they had, there was a few sawmills around here. And then the CC camps, you know, come in about that time, you know, they gave a lot of work. Like you had a CC camp up there in Big Meadows out of Troy, they had, they must have had about 150 after that. And they had another CC camp up by Moscow there, right around the Moscow and the flat there. And then they had one by Pullman and that gave a lot of work or, you know, they could make it there, you know. I don't know if they made the money, but anyhow they had a job and they made it, you know, by working a little. They made roads up in the mountains, developed springs and different things. And then everybody burned wood and they said it was quite a bit of work cutting wood, you know, cork wood, other wood. So I used to have around, oh, 10 to 12, 13 woodcutters, some of them, cutting cork wood. I sold a lot of cork wood in CC camps. I was one year there, I sold 900 cork as a cork wood. I sold about 500 cork to that CC camp out of Moscow. I sold some to one of the Pullman. And down in the Genesee they had a kind of a soil conservation camp down there.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And they all burned wood for heat, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you supply men with the tools to cut wood? Is that it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No, it was kind of a contract. I think I paid them a dollar and a half a quarter to cut and then they had their own tools. They used to have a hand saw and an ax and a sledge and a wedge. That was about it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you contracted with the CC camps?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, and I had a contract with them, you know.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, they had it out on Bitz, actually, out of Spokane, out of what they called Port Wright. That's where they, that was the head office for all the CC camps around here. So they advertised for bids for so much wood, you know, cork wood. So I went up there and bid, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, did many of the farmers just plumb up and quit around here and leave the country? Did that happen at that time?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, not so many, no, not very many.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They stuck it out?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Some of them did, some did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about foreclosing them or losing their land?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, there was some of that. But actually, you know, the banks really didn't want the land either, you know. They had a mortgage on the farm, they didn't want the farm and the insurance companies used to have quite a lot of mortgages around there. But they didn't want them either, if they could possibly, they tried to tell the man I had it by you, stay there and do the best you can and pay us what you can. They didn't want them.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And some of them left anyhow because they figured what, they was just going to get the worst off all the time. So they left the farm, let it go.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, I know, I bought that place up there by the side of Troy. Whatever's insurance, man, come over there one day, Hancock Insurance. He was telling me he thought they'd have to take over a place over on Burtridge Farm there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I said, why don't you buy it? Why don't you buy that farm?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And he said, I just bought a place there, I haven't got to hand it to my name. Well, I said, if you agree to buy it and operate it, you won't need any money. So he told me. But I really didn't want it. The way it was, you just couldn't make anything, you know, you used to load yourself down. And with a lot of farming in the fall of the year, you didn't know whether you were going to be in the red or even or what, you know. So I didn't want it really. I should have bought it, sure. If I had known what happened later, of course I took it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what about this first place that you bought? Well, how did that come about?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, that was the Walter Olson place up there by Troy, that was the first one I bought. Well, Walt let that go, or was going to let it go on the mortgage. Follow me, Mr. Ruble had a mortgage on that for about $6 ,000 and then Walter owed them about five or six hundred interest. And he, Walter was going to quit it, let it go. And that was about the time that, well, Nick Olson up here, he died, you know. And I had that place down there in the canyon, I farmed that for fifteen years down there. Well, then John Way, and he came over from Norway, and he found this up here, well, then about the time the tractor start coming in, you know. So he, he want to buy a catch, you know, and then I said I didn't have enough land, and he said he'd like to have that place down there, you know. So, I had to give that up, well, then I didn't have any land, you see, I had to quit or find another place somewhere.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So I didn't know about the South Hill, but I found the grains that I wanted to even down in Troy. And I told Clarence Johnson, I think it was, that I, it looked like I had to quit farming and lose my place down there. Well, he says, why don't you go up and buy Walter Olson, he says he's going to let it go, to Strouble on the mortgage. And he said, I'm sure if you go up there and talk to him, maybe give him a few hundred dollars above the mortgage, well, I will sell it to you. But he said, you better hurry, he says, because, because there Strouble has already made a rig and has ran it, and they're going to move in some machinery there. So, well, I thought, well, I'll come on out the next morning, you know, and talk to Walter about it, and I says I'll, I'll give you seventy two hundred for it, I says.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, he says, give me a day, he says, and I'll see what I can do. So I said, okay.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So, the next day, you know, I come around, he says, he says, you can have it, I made arrangements. He said, they already moved the disc over here, said they're going to farm it, but I talked about it, he says. So this way he got, you know, a few hundred dollars over, letting it go, just let it go on the mortgage, you see, ended up with a few hundred dollars on his own. So I bought it.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that was the first land that I bought. That was in 1935.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a holding of a mortgage by another person like Strouble did? Was this usual? Was this often what was done?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: There was a lot of that, them days, yeah, that individuals, you know, some individuals had money and rather than have the money in the bank, they tried to, you know, get it out on the mortgage on land. They got a little bit of interest that way, you know. I think this is about, I don't know what that interest was, five, five or six percent or something, and if you put it in the bank, you only get, I think they paid four at that time, and they paid four at the banks. So most of them had a little money preferred to get it out on the mortgage, got a little more interest and used it as safe, you know, maybe a little safer than having the bank too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did the, did the farm, was it easier for farmers to get a mortgage from another individual than it was to get it from the bank, let's say?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, well, no, not exactly.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I know when I bought that place up there, I didn't have any money, so, so, well, I had a little, but I couldn't get ahold of it. I think I had a couple of thousand dollars or something on a mortgage, I think, at some other place, but I couldn't get ahold of it, so I got all the money from the bank to buy it. But, just on my note, I never did have to give a mortgage down there at the bank, never did, I always got what I needed on my note. So, but they said then that we'd like for you to get some private money if you can and then pay us off, or pay part of it back, anyhow. So I said I'll do that, I'll try to do that, so, so then there's an old guy up there, Gord, he lived up there, close. Well, he lived down by the road there where Jack Thrasmus lives now, and he had a little money, he used to lend money out to the farm mortgages, and so I went and talked to him. He says, I'll have some money coming in pretty soon, he says, I'll let you have some money on that farm, take a mortgage on it. So I don't know what I got from him, four or five thousand dollars, I think. So I took that down, paid off that much in the bank.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And then those mortgages, they only set up for so many years, you know. You didn't have to pay anything off of the principal, you just paid the interest. Nowadays it's a little bit, most of the time I set up, so you pay something off of the principal and the interest every year, but they didn't have that much nowadays. You just paid the interest and the mortgage was set up then for three or four or five years, whatever it was, and then maybe you had to pay it off, you know. Maybe, of course, some of them probably renew it again. So I got that from Gord, then bought in, in, in, uh, 37, I think it was. Then things started picking up a little, I had some beans, I had pretty good bean crop, and I think the price of beans went up, so I had a little surplus money there, so. So I'd like to pay, pay Gord off some, you know, part of it. Now he says, just let it go the way it is, he says, he didn't want to accept it.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So I had to let it go up until the time ran out of the mortgage before I could pay it off. Yeah. Yessir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how did you come to get to buy some of that land from Potlatch?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, I was in the depression, you know, I bought, I started buying up there in, I think about 1934 or five, yeah, I didn't know, 1934 or five. When they had put all their land for sale them days, you know, and you could really do pretty good bargaining with them too, they, they, they wouldn't sell it. And they quit paying taxes on a lot of it them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come they wanted to sell it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, they were hard up, they were hard up for money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the long range, the timber was worth good money.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, but I tell you, they didn't believe, old Sagerston, he used to be in Atlanta, you know, and Potlatch, they had the office, it wasn't Potlatch then, you know. Not down in New York, no, now they got it in San Francisco. And they, they, they kind of believed that the timber price or the price of lumber would never come back. On account of their use in so many substitutes when they were building, you know. That was about the time that this sheetrock come in, like they all use on the inside, well I got it here, everybody got it, you know. Instead of a board, you use that. Then the cement blocks was coming in about that time and they were building with cement blocks, you know. And then of course brick, well brick they always had that, you know. And then a lot of other byproducts, you know, plaster, you know, and all that, and they, they kind of believed that the price of lumber wouldn't come back, it'd stay down, it'd be built for something else. And I think that's one reason why they, they didn't take much of the timber, of course they had lots of timber then they should have lots of timber. And of course most of this land they let go had been logged a little, you know, took the best out, the choice, stuff. But their policy actually to begin with, you know, they'd log a piece of ground, then they'd offer it for sale, for selling. They always done that, afforded the pressure.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: So by doing the Depression they let some of it go, they had lots of timber on it too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So this land was just on the open market?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yes, on the open market.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made you be interested in it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, up in there in the white pine country I got into cattle, you know, and I lived down there. I had some pasture down the canyon so I got into cattle quite a bit. And then, well there was nothing for me to eat down that canyon in the summertime, it dried up, you know, and up in the woods they'd stay green, you know. So I was actually looking for some place to run my cattle in the summer months. Well there was a guy named John Roane, he used to run up in there and then he got old, you know, and quit the cattle. So he says, no, no you better go up there and get that rig that I've used. I said I'm quitting, he says I don't need it anymore. So I did, I went up there and ran it alone. Well then I started buying, I liked that country up there, I always did really like it up there, meadows and timber and all that. I'd go up there in the summer, you know, be some nice and fresh up in there. So then I started buying, whenever I had a chance, you know, I kept buying up in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that was all owned by Potlatch, that white pine country?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Most of it, not all. Like the meadow, I don't know if you've been up in there or what, that meadow where the old mill site was there, that belonged to the Troy Lumber Company. I bought that from them, but putting all the rest in, there was another place, the Rooberg place, that Potlatch didn't have down the meadow quite a ways, I bought that.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: But nearly all the rest of it in there, well no I bought another 80 there from, well that had belonged to Potlatch, I bought it from them heights at Moscow. I think he bought it from Potlatch and then he kind of wanted to sell it. So I bought it from, well he cut cordwood up in there, you know, and I said you can cut cordwood up here now, I'll buy it from, well I'll let you cut wood in here. Not the big nice trees for logs, but I was seeing other trees sitting out of black pine, and I said you can cut all the wood you want. So I bought it, he kept the cottonwood up in there quite a few years after that, and then finally got to old. But nearly all the land up in there, it used to be Potlatch land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well they didn't want to pay taxes, how did they get it?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh I mean as far as the price that they were charging for.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh what they sell it for?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh some of them 160s up in there I bought for around $500-$600 for 160 acres.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And it would worry some then, I had a Chevy agency down in Troy there for a couple of years, they were 34, 35. So I traded my new Chevy truck for some land up there once.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And I should have bought it all one time, and he says why don't you buy all of that in here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: He says I'll put a price on all of it, buy it all. So he sent me a price list of all the land I had up in there. Oh they had a price around 200, 250 for 40s, then it had egged up a little bit. But I was asked for the matters, I was thinking about pasture and cattle, I wasn't thinking about timber, because at that time timber really wasn't worth anything, you couldn't hardly sell it. And if you did sell anything they wouldn't take anything but a perfect tree, they wouldn't have no limbs on it, it was straight, it had to be perfect. They wouldn't take white pine and some cedar, they never touched that white fir you know, them days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you told me you were pretty conservative about what you would be willing to buy anyway, you were very careful.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, I was pretty careful, well that's it you see, if I had been a little more and I hadn't been some conservative I probably would have bought a whole, kind of went in debt and bought a whole lot more. But I was scared of that depression you know, I thought maybe I'd have another one. I didn't want to go in debt much of the time, I'm going there a little. Kind of get that little down and maybe I buy a little more and go in a little more again, that's the way I kept the gold. I didn't go in pretty deep at any time you know, scared to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean, this, you buy one place at a time, real?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Yeah, that's the way I've done it, I bought one place and went in debt and then I get it paid on and then maybe paid off and then maybe I buy another one, go in again a little, that's the way I kept it. In fact I was in debt a little most of the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I bet Potlatch if they had to do it over again wouldn't sell an acre of that land the way they did. Oh, you couldn't pry, I mean I'll tell you, don't let it leak out but I'll tell you something.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I got a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was for cattle, when you were first doing it, where did you market them for one thing?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well, at that time, like cattle, most of them shipped Spokane from here then. We didn't have the spargets here like the auction, like in Lewiston. So most of them were, you know, shipped carload lots mostly to Spokane, to armor or sweat, to car steam, or to the yards you know, the stockyards in Spokane. Then you had commission companies that would sell them for you instead of selling them the auction way like they do now, they had commission men that would, in the yards that would take bids, you know, from whoever they could and the highest bidder you know, get them. And then we had a packing house in Mossquat, and Hagen and Cushing, and we'd sell packed cattle there, that's about all they bought, you know, packed cattle ready for slaughter. I sold quite a bit to them. And I sold quite a lot of cattle out in Pullman to my neighbor Hamilton, they had a meat market in Pullman, a little slaughterhouse. I drove quite a lot of cattle from here over to Pullman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah. Yeah, we drove. We'd only take two days, we'd get, we'd go south to Mossquat, they had an old road they called the Sand Road there, and we'd burn down the past Mossquaways out there, and then we'd get permission to somebody that would keep them overnight, maybe an old barnyard or someplace fancy. We'd stop overnight there and then go in with them next morning.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you ride horseback?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, we rode horseback. Old time cattle driving. Oh yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: One time we drove, heard from here down to Clarkston. Yeah, I think we had about 85 or 90 here, that was Oberg, a fellow named Genesee and I. Well we bought some cattle, I didn't have that many of them, he bought some and I bought some. And we drove to Genesee, Oberg had a place, they lived in Genesee, and he had about 20 acres there close to town. So we drove them over there and we held them there a few days, so we got buyers to come up there and bid on them. We didn't want to take them in the party, we had them sold. So we got buyers there, an old fellow named Sweet Nelson, he come up and made us offers, and then there's another guy, Taylor, and he come up and gave us offers. So we finally sold them there and then this guy we sold them to, he said you got to deliver them down there across the bridge, in Clarkston, and I'll meet you there and take over. So that's what we done. We drove, we'd go up in the normal hill, and then take them across normal hill, and then down on Snake River and down to the bridge, and we got, we made arrangements with the policemen down there to stop all the traffic on the bridge while we drove them across.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh yeah, it went alright. Yeah, but I'll never forget that night. We rode back to Genesee that night, and it rained and the wind blew, there was old Herman Boyd and I, Oberg, he stayed down there I think that night. And old Herman Boyd and I, we rode back that night, and boy oh boy did we get soaked, just there wasn't a dry thread down the shore. We rode down the water just back there to Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many head would you be having in those days when you were having a canyon here?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh I used to run around a hundred head, cabin.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do with them in the summer before you had the white pine?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well I didn't have that many before I guess, no. No I didn't have that many until I got some summer ends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they easy to keep track of back in there on the white pine?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh not too bad, I used to salt them there and go up there once in a while, kind of call them in you know and salt them and they didn't stray off bad, they stayed pretty good.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: I actually didn't lose many there to start with. Once in a while there'd be some maybe getting over on the other side, over on the Platte Creek side. Well some of their cattle come over on the white pine too, a few not too many, old Sam Crumley used to have a few cows that got started coming over there and they'd come over every summer, them same cows. Well then in the fall they'd go home again you know. Well maybe I had some yearlings would get in with them and they'd follow them cows over there on the other side, so I had to go over there a few times and get them and bring them back. But later on toward the last stop in there, they got so loose a few once in a while.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: What happened to them really?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: And everybody else did, everybody else got loose a few.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Sounds pretty suspicious to me. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Oh there was some rustling going on all over the country, really is today I guess. It's actually getting worse you know, all the time. But it wasn't something that was here in the early days, it's something that came in later probably. Yeah it wasn't so much when I first started going up in there, there was a few bad eggs around the home.

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: Well then you come in with trucks you know and one thing or another and they made a little issue for them guys you know, they could slip in the night and get one or two in the truck and take off and by morning they could be two or three hundred miles away from here you know. They had some place to take them to get rid of them right away, pretty hard. Wouldn't have got to catch them in the act.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have somebody with the herd at all during the summer?

speaker: Edward Ramsdale
words: No I really never had any herders much, no. No I just turned them up in there and I got them in there quite often myself. Then I had some woodcutters up in there most of the time that kind of washed around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think has been the biggest change, changes in the country?
